author: @stevetobias4890
text: Detergent works but you get the same result with a finger dipped in water.

author: @edgeeffect
text: I'm totally incompetent with silicone... so I'll give this a go.

author: @hamishanderson8619
text:
Push it , don't pull , it forces the silicone in where you need it and leaves much less behind to clean up afterwards. 
Also on straight runs , set a border with masking tape , pull it off at right  angles and as flat as possible to your bead , before it set's , leaves very clean joints. Nice video Luke!

author: @greggrimer1428
text: I have a better idea Luke. Assuming the silicone is to hold those fans in.  Though what is the point in 'sealing' them to the bucket lid when there is a hole in the middle of the fan?  Mark where the fan touches the lid put a small bead 3 or 4mm inside the line and carefully drop the fan onto that silicone bead.  It then stick the fan to the lid and looks better than the mess you had.  Does not need a finger run around it.

author: @aomanchutube

author: @deemdoubleu
text: Trying not to be negative but while this works in your application, I wouldn't seal around my bath using this method.  Perhaps the title of this video should be how to apply silicone properly in this particular application.  Best way I have found for conventional application is to use one of the silicone profiling tools which you can buy pretty much anywhere nowadays.

Thanks for your videos!

This is not a complaint, just a reflection/idea; A future project could be acoustic dampeners for the rooms you record in. I have seen a lot of takes on this and would not mind seeing another one. It is not crucial or anything, but kind of like a cherry on top ;)

author: @chadjoseph5810
text: terrible form

author: @pencilguin
text: Could you demonstrate how you would say 'Silicon' (the chemical element) versus how you say 'Silicone'? In the US, the last syllable of silicone rhymes with 'own'. Also, many people pronounce the element Silicon as rhyming with 'on', but sometimes it is similar to the way you are saying silicone (to my ears). If you would say both, then I would have a better idea how to hear what you are saying. Thanks!

author: @1upPaintingAB
text: I just use water and clean bare hands rather than gloves and soap. I wipe off excess from my finger onto a wet rag. They key is keeping surdaces clean and a little scuffing with synthetic wool helps bonding also, especially on plastics.

author: @totherarf
text: Spitty finger is the pro's choice, ......... but if you have straight lengths an old credit card works well too ;0)

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Hahaha I have never heard of this method until today, the pros choice I like that a lot lol! Cheers mate! :)

author: @maximusdarja
text: I have heard that dish soap acts as a catalyst for the type of silicon that comes in the contractors tubes.  If you look around the internet you can see a lot of people making one-off molds of small objects that self cure in 45 or so mins.  They mix watered down dish soap into the silicon to accelerate the cure.  It's not just about the non stick. :D

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Wow, I didn't know that! It's super interesting. Thanks mate! :)

replies.author: @michaeldean5787
replies.text: There's also a way of speed drying it by mixing it with equal parts cornflour/cornstarch so it can be used like sugru

author: @GigsTaggart
text: You have no idea the pandora's box you've opened here.  People on youtube have strong opinions about the proper way to apply silicone.

replies.author: @maximusdarja
replies.text: It's ok, people can be wrong all they want.  You should use a LPHV gun while standing on a carousel and that's the only way.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Whoops lol!

author: @ThisTall
text: I’ve seen plumbers use a spray bottle of soapy water. After they lay their bead they spray it, then it’s basically Dap and can’t stick to anything it wasn’t touching before you sprayed it.

author: @nattsurfaren
text: I would be nervous like hell filming myself + say things in one swoop without errors + holding, showing, handling without error + smiling while talking. This is really really hard. If you can do this without any retakes then you're a pro. Awesome Job!!!!

replies.author: @scotttovey
replies.text:
Your classification of pro does not exist. It's why major YouTubers will from time to time post their out takes. 

The purpose of including outtakes at the end is to show those just starting out, or wanting to do vlogging that even the "experts" make mistakes. Just redo the spot until you get it right.

If you think that's unusual; I believe the DVD version of Cannon Ball Run has bloopers during the credits. The actors can't always get their lines right, so instead of fretting over them, they choose to laugh and have fun with the mistakes.

Sometimes; actors intentionally make a mistake in order to trip up their co-star.

replies.author: @nattsurfaren
@scotttovey
replies.text:
@nattsurfaren 
Perhaps you are covering the wrong subject or just using the wrong technique.
Start with the subject you have the most knowledge of and enjoy the most. 
Use bullet points rather than a script, that way your speaking off the cuff rather than following a script. This also makes it easier to talk to the camera as if you are conversing with someone. That way; you're less likely to trip over your words.
How often do you have to repeat yourself when you are engaged in a conversation?

replies.author: @nattsurfaren
replies.text:
@scotttovey Hey thanks for your encouraging words. I use a technique that works for me. Because I'm Swedish I frequently mispronounce English words. I don't film myself because it is not a format I'm comfortable with. I just keep things simple. I simply record small video segments and if I do one wrong I just repeat. It doesn't really show in the video because I use screen recording in all my videos so you won't see a face that suddenly changes position or expression. That is the benefit of recording a screen. Then I simply stitch the videos together. Another issue is keeping a high enough tempo so people don't fall asleep. Because of that I stumble on the words and need to slow myself down to get through what I need to say.  
If I'm not wrong, the video you recorded I believe you didn't do any retakes. You seemed relaxed and did it very well.

replies.author: @scotttovey
replies.text:
@nattsurfaren 
"I don't film myself because it is not a format I'm comfortable with. I just keep things simple. I simply record small video segments and if I do one wrong I just repeat."

That's the way many creators on YouTube do it.
Vlog Boss Amy Landino uses it.
I believe Sean Cannell and Benji Travis of Video Influencers use it when they are not doing an interview. 
It certainly won't work for a live stream

It's how motion pictures are made as well.
Each scene in a movie is shot and then everything is stitched together in post processing.
It also allows for creators to add blooper segments to the end of the video.

"If I'm not wrong, the video you recorded I believe you didn't do any retakes. You seemed relaxed and did it very well."

You are correct, it was a single take. But then, I was speaking on a subject that no one other than myself knew and could do so from memory. I figured it from the stand point of preaching a message. You get no do overs when you're standing at a pulpit in front of a crowd. So, if you trip up, you trip up. You just make sure  you correct yourself when you do.

The only people who care about someone on the stage correcting themselves after misspeaking are individuals that need not be taken serious or bothered with once they open their mouth. Anyone that thinks they can do it without making mistakes has probably not even attempted it.
